findings.description: Header layout collapsed from two columns to one.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the header features a two-column layout with department information on the left and leadership names on the right, separated by a vertical line. In the HTML, this is rendered as a single column with the leadership names stacked below the department information.

findings.description: Table headers split and rows collapsed.
findings.evidence: In both tables on the PDF, header cells span multiple columns (e.g., 'Timeliness of Provider Payment', 'March', 'April') and the top-left labels are split across two rows ('Audit Type' / 'LME/MCO' or 'Month' / 'LME/MCO'). In the HTML tables, the spanning headers are incorrectly split into single columns with duplicated text (e.g., 'March' | 'March'), and the first two rows are collapsed into a single row (e.g., 'Month LME/MCO').

findings.description: Table headers split and rows collapsed.
findings.evidence: Similar to page 2, the PDF tables feature month headers (e.g., 'March', 'April') that span two columns, and the top-left header is structured across two rows ('Month' / 'LME/MCO'). The HTML conversion splits these spanning headers into individual columns with duplicated text and collapses the first two header rows into one (e.g., 'Month LME/MCO').

findings.description: Table headers spanning multiple columns in the PDF are represented in the HTML by duplicating the header text across adjacent cells rather than using the `colspan` attribute.
findings.evidence: In the PDF's Solvency Review tables, the month headers (e.g., 'March') visually span two columns (for the numeric value and the 'Yes/No' result). In the HTML, this is coded as `<th>March</th><th>March</th>`. Data is fully preserved, but the structural implementation is slightly repetitive.
